description:
You have been called in as an incident responder to quickly identify what user-accounts
    have experienced failed log-ins on a suspect host.

    1. A SQLite database file already exists at  
       /home/user/ir_case/logs.db

    2. Inside that database there is a single table named **auth** with the exact schema  
       (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, ts TEXT, user TEXT, status TEXT).

    3. Your objective is to extract an alphabetically-sorted list of every
       DISTINCT user value whose status is the string literal **'FAIL'**
       (case-sensitive), and save it to a CSV file with **one single terminal
       command that uses the sqlite3 CLI**.

    4. The output CSV **must** be created at  
       /home/user/ir_case/failed_users.csv

    5. The CSV must be in canonical SQLite “-csv” format produced by the
       sqlite3 CLI **with headers turned on**:
       • Column separator must be a single comma (`,`) with no surrounding spaces.  
       • UNIX line endings (`\n`).  
       • The very first line must be the header **user** in lowercase.  
       • Each following line must contain exactly one username.  
       • The usernames must appear in strict alphabetical order (case-sensitive,
         ASCII sort).

       Example of the required structure (DO NOT infer the real usernames from
       this sample; it only illustrates the format):

           user
           alice
           bob
           charlie

    6. When you are done, the file /home/user/ir_case/failed_users.csv should
       exist and follow the format rules above.  
       No extra columns, no quotation marks, no blank lines, no trailing spaces.

    7. Use only a single sqlite3 invocation in the terminal; shell redirection
       is allowed.  
       Do not edit the database; only read from it.

    The automated test will:
       • Confirm that /home/user/ir_case/failed_users.csv exists.  
       • Verify that the file content is byte-for-byte identical to the expected
         CSV (header + usernames in order).  
       • Ensure the file was generated from a single sqlite3 CLI command.

    You do not need elevated privileges. Everything is writable by the
    current user.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing artefacts -->
    <initial>
        <directory path="/home/user/ir_case" mode="0755"/>
        <file path="/home/user/ir_case/logs.db" mode="0644" type="sqlite">
            <!-- Schema -->
            CREATE TABLE auth(
                id      INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
                ts      TEXT,
                user    TEXT,
                status  TEXT
            );
            <!-- Data -->
            INSERT INTO auth VALUES
                (1,'2023-08-01 11:23:31','alice','OK'),
                (2,'2023-08-01 11:24:02','bob','FAIL'),
                (3,'2023-08-01 11:25:15','charlie','FAIL'),
                (4,'2023-08-01 11:26:45','alice','FAIL'),
                (5,'2023-08-01 11:28:17','dave','OK'),
                (6,'2023-08-01 11:30:00','charlie','FAIL'),
                (7,'2023-08-01 11:32:12','eve','FAIL'),
                (8,'2023-08-01 11:34:05','bob','OK'),
                (9,'2023-08-01 11:35:19','frank','FAIL');
        </file>
    </initial>

    <!-- Expected artefacts after agent completion -->
    <expected>
        <file path="/home/user/ir_case/failed_users.csv" mode="0644" type="text">
user
alice
bob
charlie
eve
frank
        </file>
    </expected>
